context: Current closing price of Gartner (IT): $154.62 USD as of 2026-04-17.
justification_text:
My point estimates reflect a modest upward drift assumption from what I'd infer as a recent baseline price. At the one-day horizon, the estimate of 155.5 is essentially anchored to the most recent observed price with a very small positive drift, assuming normal market continuity and no major overnight catalysts. At the one-week horizon, the slightly higher estimate of 157.0 incorporates a gentle trend extrapolation, assuming the asset has mild positive momentum or a slight fundamental tailwind. At the one-month horizon, 160.0 reflects a continued but modest upward trajectory, consistent with either a slowly improving fundamental backdrop or mean-reversion dynamics if the asset had recently dipped. In all three cases, I was not assuming any dramatic repricing event, just a steady, gradual appreciation that might characterize an equity or commodity in a relatively stable environment.

The confidence interval widths widen appropriately with time, reflecting the square-root-of-time scaling of uncertainty that is standard in financial modeling. At one day, the 90% interval of 148 to 163 is fairly tight, capturing typical daily volatility. By one week, the interval expands to 143–171, acknowledging that intraweek news flow, earnings, or macro data releases could meaningfully shift the price in either direction. At one month, the interval of 133–188 is considerably wider, as a full month introduces exposure to central bank decisions, geopolitical developments, sector rotations, and earnings cycles. The asymmetry is relatively modest across all horizons, suggesting I was not pricing in a strong skew toward either a crash or a melt-up scenario at the time of the forecast.

Several specific risks could push outcomes well outside these stated intervals. On the downside, a surprise deterioration in macroeconomic data, a sudden tightening of financial conditions, or a geopolitical shock could easily breach the lower bounds, particularly at the one-week and one-month horizons. A liquidity event or sector-specific negative catalyst could accelerate selling beyond normal volatility assumptions. On the upside, an unexpected positive earnings surprise, a dovish pivot from a major central bank, or a short-squeeze dynamic could push prices above the upper bounds. These tail risks are by definition underweighted in a symmetric confidence interval framework, meaning the true probability of extreme outcomes may be slightly higher than the 5% tails I implied on each side.
